description:
You are acting as an on-call incident responder who has just received two potentially suspicious log files that have already been collected for you.  
Your job is to organise the evidence, create a repeatable chain-of-custody package, and document the first findings in a very precise way so that another responder (or an automated system) can immediately verify your work.

Pre-existing evidence  
1. /home/user/logs/http-access-incident.log  
2. /home/user/logs/firewall-20240614.log  

Goal  
Create a brand-new response workspace at /home/user/incident_response that contains ALL of the artefacts listed below and *only* in the exact structure shown.  The entire job can be completed with roughly 5-10 terminal commands.

Required final layout (directories and files)

/home/user/incident_response
├── evidence/                        # directory – copies of original logs ONLY
│   ├── http-access-incident.log
│   └── firewall-20240614.log
├── evidence.tgz                     # gzip-compressed tar archive of **both** logs;
│                                    # the archive itself must contain the two files
│                                    # at its top level, no extra directory prefixes.
├── digest.sha256                    # SHA-256 checksums for each artefact
│                                    # (three lines: one for each log + one for evidence.tgz)
└── report/
    └── findings.csv                 # structured summary of the suspicious events

Very strict file-format requirements

A. digest.sha256  
   • Plain text, Unix line-endings.  
   • Exactly three lines and nothing else.  
   • Each line must be the normal output format of the `sha256sum` utility, i.e.  
     "<64-char hexadecimal hash><two spaces><filename>\n"  
   • The filenames in the digest file must appear **exactly** as they are stored on disk:  
     http-access-incident.log, firewall-20240614.log, evidence.tgz  
   • Example of the required pattern (hash shown as X’s here):  
     XXXXX…  http-access-incident.log  

B. findings.csv  
   • CSV with header + four data rows (total five lines).  
   • File must use *exactly* this header, all lowercase with commas and no spaces:  
     timestamp,logfile,src_ip,dst_ip,action,notes  
   • The four data rows must be written in this order:  
     1. 14/Jun/2024:02:15:23,http-access-incident.log,192.168.1.50,-,404,"Attempt to access /admin/login.php"  
     2. 14/Jun/2024:02:16:01,http-access-incident.log,203.0.113.77,-,200,"Potential brute force on /wp-login.php"  
     3. 14/Jun/2024:02:15:24,firewall-20240614.log,203.0.113.77,192.168.1.10,BLOCK,"Blocked TCP SYN to port 80"  
     4. 14/Jun/2024:02:16:10,firewall-20240614.log,198.51.100.23,192.168.1.10,BLOCK,"Blocked TCP SYN to port 22"  
   • End the file with a single newline after the last row.

Checks that the automated grader will perform

1. Directory /home/user/incident_response exists with exactly the four items shown above.  
2. The evidence sub-directory holds byte-for-byte copies of the two original log files.  
3. evidence.tgz is a valid gzip file; when extracted it yields exactly the two log files at the archive’s root.  
4. digest.sha256 contains three valid SHA-256 hashes whose file portions exactly match the three artefacts; the grader will recompute the hashes and make sure they match.  
5. report/findings.csv exists and its five lines match the specification (header + 4 rows), with correct comma placement, quotation marks and trailing newline.  

Hints (do NOT list commands)  
• Remember to create parent directories as needed.  
• Use cp for copying files, tar with the correct flags to control path names, and sha256sum to generate the hashes.  
• Build findings.csv manually or with shell utilities, but respect the required order and formatting.

When everything is finished, the automated test suite should be able to untar evidence.tgz, recompute the SHA-256 sums and compare them, and parse findings.csv without any difference.
truth:
Initial files that already exist before the agent starts:

1. File: /home/user/logs/http-access-incident.log  
   Contents (including the trailing newline after each line):  
   192.168.1.50 - - [14/Jun/2024:02:15:23 +0000] "GET /admin/login.php HTTP/1.1" 404 560 "-" "Mozilla/5.0"\n  
   203.0.113.77 - - [14/Jun/2024:02:16:01 +0000] "POST /wp-login.php HTTP/1.1" 200 482 "-" "Mozilla/5.0"\n  
   198.51.100.23 - - [14/Jun/2024:02:16:22 +0000] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 762 "-" "curl/7.68.0"\n  

2. File: /home/user/logs/firewall-20240614.log  
   Contents (including the trailing newline after each line):  
   Jun 14 02:15:24 host kernel: [UFW BLOCK] IN=eth0 OUT= MAC=00:0c:29:4f:8e:35 SRC=203.0.113.77 DST=192.168.1.10 LEN=60 TOS=0x00 PREC=0x00 TTL=47 ID=54321 DF PROTO=TCP SPT=55932 DPT=80 WINDOW=29200 RES=0x00 SYN URGP=0\n  
   Jun 14 02:16:10 host kernel: [UFW BLOCK] IN=eth0 OUT= MAC=00:0c:29:4f:8e:35 SRC=198.51.100.23 DST=192.168.1.10 LEN=60 TOS=0x00 PREC=0x00 TTL=49 ID=12345 DF PROTO=TCP SPT=49000 DPT=22 WINDOW=29200 RES=0x00 SYN URGP=0\n  

Expected content created by the agent:

A. /home/user/incident_response/evidence/http-access-incident.log  
   -> identical to original

B. /home/user/incident_response/evidence/firewall-20240614.log  
   -> identical to original

C. /home/user/incident_response/report/findings.csv  
   Lines (each terminated by \n):  
   timestamp,logfile,src_ip,dst_ip,action,notes\n  
   14/Jun/2024:02:15:23,http-access-incident.log,192.168.1.50,-,404,"Attempt to access /admin/login.php"\n  
   14/Jun/2024:02:16:01,http-access-incident.log,203.0.113.77,-,200,"Potential brute force on /wp-login.php"\n  
   14/Jun/2024:02:15:24,firewall-20240614.log,203.0.113.77,192.168.1.10,BLOCK,"Blocked TCP SYN to port 80"\n  
   14/Jun/2024:02:16:10,firewall-20240614.log,198.51.100.23,192.168.1.10,BLOCK,"Blocked TCP SYN to port 22"\n  

D. /home/user/incident_response/digest.sha256  
   • Three lines, hashes will be calculated during test execution; the grader will check format and verify that the digest matches recomputed SHA-256 sums of:  
     - evidence/http-access-incident.log  
     - evidence/firewall-20240614.log  
     - evidence.tgz  

E. /home/user/incident_response/evidence.tgz  
   • A valid gzip stream; tar -tzf should list exactly:  
     http-access-incident.log  
     firewall-20240614.log  

The grader will:

1. Confirm that findings.csv exists with exactly the five lines shown above (byte for byte).  
2. Recalculate SHA-256 sums for the two copied logs and the tarball; confirm digest.sha256 matches.  
3. Extract evidence.tgz and verify that:  
   - No extra directories appear in the archive.  
   - The internal files match the original log contents.  
4. Ensure no extraneous files or directories were created inside /home/user/incident_response except the ones defined.
